logs.name: rank-convolution
logs.text:
>It's just a costume.

So then there's nothing wrong with going to a bar mitzvah dressed as a Nazi. It's just a costume after all.

logs.name: meek-bight
logs.text:
You have to define what cultural appropriation *is*. Your definition seems to imply that it means nothing more than, say, a Chinese guy opening a pizzeria. That is, someone using something from a culture not their "own," and running with it. This is fine, it always has been and always will be.

However, this is not, from my understanding, what 'cultural appropriation' is. The problem comes when you perform the above, but in a manner that is *exploitative* and *disrespectful* to the source material.

By way of explanation, a good example is a Halloween costume that makes you look like a native American chieftain. It seems innocent, until you consider the historical background for those people and what a chieftain is.

For starters, a chieftain isn't just some guy with a funny headdress -- he's a political leader and VIP among his people. He's the equivalent of a president or king in terms of power and stature. But the costume you get at the store *reduces* that entire station to nothing more than some cheap fabric and some face paint.

And, more to the point, *who is this disrespectful parody marketed towards?* The people who *stole* the land from the thing they're mocking. Well, not the people themselves, but the descendants thereof who have benefited directly. It's also ignoring the breadth and depth of entire swathes of native American culture (much of which we don't know because it was *destroyed*, deliberately) and just presenting a single facet as if that facet were the only thing worthy of note.

This is what differentiates 'cultural appropriation' from just 'borrowing' an idea. A Chinese guy can go to Nepal, learn their recipes, return home, open a restaurant and as long as he gives credit to the source of inspiration, no one will mind. He can even, over time, develop his own 'inspired' recipes, so long as he acknowledges the source of his inspiration.

But if he does all that and claims the recipes as his own, or presents them as a mocking caricature of their actual forms ("this is what those people really eat!") and deliberately profits off of other peoples work and traditions, that's not okay. If he were to reduce a rich cultural background to a few, simple, token meals, that wouldn't be okay. *Especially* when you consider the history China and Nepal have had.

It's the component of disrespect, exploitation and reductionism (even if those things aren't intentional) that makes cultural appropriation, cultural appropriation. Humans have always and *should* always share ideas because it benefits us all. But not when those ideas are *used* for a selfish, hurtful end.

logs.name: quantum-watchtower
logs.text: Wow, this is a really important distinction. I hadn't really thought about it that way. It makes sense to think about intent and impact. @meek-bight you've hit on something really insightful here. I guess I was too quick to jump to conclusions about costumes. Thanks for laying this out so clearly.
